reviews.review_text: This game is very good. Certainly the best gameplay in the entire Metal Gear series, perhaps even the best gameplay in any game I've ever played. The complete freedom that you have means you can approach missions in any way you like, utilising a wide variety of weapons and gadgets. The movement is very fluid and you can pretty much do whatever you want. The open world is okay. There's a lot to do but somehow also feels empty at times. The level design, although really good, can feel tiring and repetitive, especially in the second act of the game where some missions are literally repeating previous missions but with a harder difficulty. However, I do believe these feelings can be mitigated quite easily simply by switching up your loadout or trying a different approach. As briefly mentioned earlier, there are well over 100 different weapons to choose from, as well as a bunch of companions who can accompany you on missions. The story, and the quality of the storytelling, is quite good in the first half and then kind of drops off in the second half as the game was unfortunately left unfinished due to Hideo Kojima's departure from Konami. A somewhat controversial decision was made to replace Snake's voice actor, David Hayter, with Kiefer Sutherland, although I believe he was an excellent fit for Venom Snake's character, as well as the more serious tone the game takes. Overall, I think this game is definitely worth playing. Story-wise, it isn't as good as the other Metal Gear games but in every other aspect, this is a brilliant game that holds up extremely well considering it was released over a decade ago.

Metal Gear Solid V is a famously unfinished mess of a game with a ridiculous story buried in optional audio tapes, that destroys any sense of continuity with the rest of the series, flattens established characters down to practically nothing, and devolves into missions so repetitive that it took me a decade to finally roll the credits, and yet there is no conceivable way I could rate it any less than a 9/10. 

The actual moment-to-moment stealth action gameplay in MGSV is practically unparalleled in terms of tools it offers and ways to approach even just free-roam messing about, without ever becoming overwhelming. Alongside probably thousands of side-objectives, it means that even among a relatively small number of locales, you can hop in and do just about whatever you feel like, regardless of what you're actually "supposed" to be doing, with some of the smoothest and most intuitive third-person action ever made. And that's with the benefit that pretty much anything you undertake feeds into the base expansion and weapons development systems that are the real marks of progression in the game. 

Sure, I'm still a little mad that we didn't get my dream game of chasing down Major Zero as he goes mad with power, explaining how in the hell the codec crew from MGS3 became the Illuminati, but it's easy to ignore that when the game is actually running. Not to mention that this will run on the most basic of modern PCs and still looks great. Simply put, MGSV is a must-play for anyone interested in stealth games even if you haven't touched the rest of the series, because it's not like playing the rest would help this thing make any more sense.

The story is a beautifully tragic mess, but the stealth action gameplay is an absolute, untouchable 10/10 masterclass. Let's be completely honest: the plot becomes a fragmented, unfinished jigsaw puzzle by Chapter 2. Kojima was locked in a corporate battle, and it shows. But if you judge this game strictly by how it feels to play, there is simply nothing else on the market that touches it. The AI adapt to your playstyle. If you always headshot them at night, they start wearing helmets and night-vision goggles. After 120 hours, I was still finding new ways to approach objectives. It's the ultimate stealth sandbox
calling pequod after a mission with fortune son blasting in the background is peak mgsv. absolute war dogs moment
It feels like you have to imagine half of the game, however this does not mean that the game is ass. This is by far the best stealth game made.

Let me start this review by stating a couple of things: 1. I had a gamebreaking bug after mission 31 where I hit the black screen of death. None of the fixes online worked for it so I’m pretty much forced to restart the game If I wanna see if I can get past it. I’m not doing that so I opted to read the rest of the story by synopsis so I missed out on some of the later mission. 2: The extent of my experience with the metal gear franchise only goes to mgs2, msg4, peace walker, and now this game; So if I missed out on anything in the story of this game then that’s the reason why. Let’s get into it the actual review

Mgs5 is probably a controversial game especially when it came out. It eschew the somewhat linear format of prior games and goes to a open world format that I think it worked really well on in term of the gameplay. The ability to approach any missions in any way that I wanted meant that I could try different things and see how it worked plus I could develop gears to fit the playstyle that I wanted. I enjoyed seeing myself getting slowly better at stealth over the course of the game. I had a lot of trouble at first until I slowly got better at understanding the a.i’s “senses” Be warned though, The game does adapt to your tactics and it will force you to become better at those strategies or invent new ones.

However, given the open world nature and the “do what you want” style of gameplay meant that the pacing of the story is entirely dictated by the player which usually mean that the pacing is all over the place.. Especially in the last half of the game (from my reading and talking to friends that beat the game). I feel like this kind of did a disservice from the usual excellent narrative control that the metal gear solid franchise has always been known for, especially since this game is a little light on cutscenes to tell you the story. Most of the story is pretty much told through background info via tapes you listen to in between missions. The tapes themselves are a cool flaw in the game’s narrative. It’s cool to get a after mission briefing but so much of the details will be lost if you don’t listen to every single one of them. That means you’re going to spend a while listening to people talk at you in a not so engaging way even compared to the radios in the other games. I ended up calling the twist because I listened to the tapes and because I played the other games. It was a neat twist tho.

Slight spoiler: [spoiler] I feel like the theme of the game doesn’t fully match up if you tend to do the non lethal route as much as possible. That’s just me knowing that the whole motivation at first was revenge against Cipher but I choose to do that in the least morally bankrupt way possible [/spoiler]

All the characters did a great job at their role. It was really cool to see what happened after peace walker and ground zero and how everyone just comes together (well those that survived).I really liked what all the companions had to offer. D-Dog is best boy with a knife. Quiet was obviously meant to be a waifu for some people no matter how much Kojima denies it but her character was interesting despite that potential pain point for some people plus she’s actually useful in the game if you fully unlock her gear. D-horse is great for traversal and some creative vehicle sabotage (if you know, you know). I didn’t use D-walker until the very end of the game (well my end of the game) but it proved to be very helpful in several ways in both offensive and stealth capability. If you maxed out all your bonds level then I’d recommend using d-walker. It’s sort of a jack of all trade plus you don’t need to air drop a vehicle since it is one itself.

Performance overall was phenomenal. The game is limited to 60 fps but there are mods that uncap that or you can uncap it yourself. I played on 3 different computers using cloud save and varying level of power. This ran well on my main gaming machine (attached), My linux living room pc with a rx7600 in it, and the steam deck. They all ran at 60 fps or higher the majority of the game.

Mgs5 is a good game. I liked the other Metal gear games more since I feel like Kojima just does linear stories a bit better but 5’s gameplay is probably the best in the series for me so that really elevate it in the ranking for me. I would easily recommend playing this game with one catch: It does not have manual save so make sure you take note of the checkpoints popping up so you don’t end up losing progress accidentally. This being a metal gear solid game, make sure you have plenty of game to play a couple of missions. This is not a game you can play for 30 minutes at a time if you want to make any progress.
Fantastic game with amazing game mechanics that appeal to many different playstyles, this truly was our Metal Gear Solid 5: The Phantom Pain

the game is amazing u can tell by me spending 121 hours on a game u can beat in 50 hours max it is incomplete due to the ending being an open based ending but it left a  huge effect on me and it will do to you too

Creativity is the most enjoyable here. Learn different ways to Neutralize, extract, or just play around with your food.

The final instalment of the legendary MGS franchise.

The sneaking and gameplay are tight. Probably the best of the entire series. Worth it for that alone, if you're a veteran of the other Metal Gear games.

Unfortunately, there are several things that are a step backward.
The online component is immersion breaking. I don't care about coins or other meaningless currency.
The Mother Base element of the game is entirely forgettable. Unnecessary bloat.
The story leaves a lot to be desired. 
I am also torn about the open world. I think I prefer the previous iterations where the environment is smaller, but more detailed.

Nonetheless, the sneaking gameplay is of such a caliber that it is worth suffering through the game's limitations. Trying to achieve the "Perfect stealth, No Kills" bonus is immensely satisfying when you succeed.

Game Devs out there, I await the spiritual successor...

Not the best story line out of the Metal Gear Solid series, but this game is probably one of the best stealth games ever made. It still holds up as an 11 year old game.

 The ways you can go about completing missions and capturing outposts are almost endless with all the different gear there is (Cardboard box FTW). You're also able to approach them from anyway you'd like. Infiltrating other peoples FOB's is pretty fun too.

 This isn't necessarily a short game, with the main story taking 40+ hours to complete. There's also optional side objectives in each mission, and 150+ side ops to complete in the open world.

[h2][b]The best and worst you can get from Kojima![/b][/h2]
Metal Gear is with me for quite some time, I even share my birth year with the first instalment of the Metal Gear Solid series. This is my last entry that endeavour and wholeheartedly I must admit that this is my least favourite main entry of the series! 
Do not get me wrong, this is still Metal Gear, another  unique take on the stealth action espionage genre, but in my honest opinion it lacks substance, more specifically content. 
The series is known for its political, war commentary, atmospheric, emotionally tragic mess, often unintentionally hilarious. However, here substantial parts were cut due to pushed release that I am aware of and the whole Konami fiasco. The game’s plot is short and there are a lot of unfinished plots in the end, although I have to praise Kojima for not forcing me into relation with [u]QUIET[/u] character, you can just skip her missions and safely arrive in closing credits without unnecessary, in my opinion, fanservice and objectification of female body. 
Miller, Ocelot, Eli Codetalker are great companions, I only wish we could have seen them more often!
The gameplay is the best it could possibly be in the series, there are TONS of toys, gadgets and ways to complete every mission, you can spend countless hours doing these and most of the time it is precious fun! 
If you want open world stealth action you will get it in its best state to date, 
but I can't fully recommend this game to fans of the series as it leaves a huge missing link in heart, whether it was intentional or not it does not show respect to the player, 
that is The Phantom Pain.

MGSV is my favorite Metal Gear game ever. It has the best stealth mechanics and a story that may not be the absolute best, but is in the top 3. The Fox Engine is also extremely efficient, not even taking up 30 GB of storage and allowing me to play the game in medium settings with the potato PC I have. I highly recommend this game.

The game is fine if you want open world stealth game and don't care about metal gear. There is a lot to do and missions are replayable. There is a ton to grind for if that's your thing.

I don't think this is the best stealth game ever and it has the classic open world barren wasteland map with POI's loosely scattered about. I don't like the way they increase difficulty throughout the game by giving enemies armor and making it harder to tranq them instead of making levels more intricate or increasing their awareness or even just increasing enemy spawns. In previous games it feels like you were encouraged to use nonlethal weapons rather than regular guns but it feels the opposite in MGSV. 

This is the worst game in the series, there is no replayability compared to the older games. The story sucks and is locked behind cassette tapes that you have to open 4 menus to get to just for it to cut off anytime a game event happens. I "finished" this game feeling so disappointed because it feels like a cash grab. Having to wait real world time for resources to pour in slowly unless I want to spend real money on virtual coins to cut down the time it takes to get said resources. Having to grind another 15-20 hours to unlock the final mission is not worth my time and I just watched it on youtube. 

I'm recommending this because I still think it is a solid 7/10 game that should be slow burned so you don't feel like you are waiting for cooldowns more than anything else. Still disappointed.

A Phantom Masterpiece: Freedom, Identity, and What Could Have Been

Metal Gear Solid V: The Phantom Pain is not the kind of game you simply finish and move on from. It is something you take in gradually, mission by mission, system by system, detail by detail. It blends tactical freedom, base management, fragmented storytelling, and a constant meditation on loss and identity. Even with the visible signs of a troubled production, Hideo Kojima’s fingerprints are all over it. And, as usual, he does not disappoint.

MGSV is a masterpiece in both gameplay and ambition. Its story works as a direct continuation of Metal Gear Solid: Peace Walker, originally released on the PSP, while also serving as a prequel to the original Metal Gear on the MSX from 1987.

The game pulls from several key moments in the franchise: the hostile military setting, Soviet presence, and Cold War echoes that bring Snake Eater to mind; the Mother Base management and progression systems from Peace Walker; the free third-person camera introduced in Subsistence and later refined into a more action-driven style in Guns of the Patriots; and the themes of identity, manipulation, and constructed reality that connect back to Sons of Liberty.

The progression system is incredibly addictive. Building up Mother Base, recruiting soldiers, upgrading gear, completing side objectives, and mastering each mission all create a constant sense of growth. It would be almost perfect if not for the commercial elements tied to the online and progression systems, such as MB Coins, which feel unnecessary in a game of this scale and quality.

Still, if you pay attention, it is clear that the core experience was carefully preserved. Even with those commercial elements in place, the main game remains strong, functional, and deeply engaging, without losing what makes Metal Gear special.

With the new direction MGSV takes, everything works extremely well, but there is still a feeling that it could have been even more refined. Not just bigger in scale, but stronger in presence. Mother Base is beautiful, massive, and visually impressive, but it never feels truly alive. It lacks more animations, interactions, activities, and a stronger sense of daily life. It could have felt almost like a military island-city, full of movement and human presence, but instead it often comes across as a huge set piece rather than an organic place. In that sense, maybe the game could have stayed closer to Peace Walker, where Mother Base lived mostly in the player’s imagination instead of needing to be physically explored. Sometimes, suggestion is more powerful than showing.

The same issue shows up in the open world. The tactical freedom is incredible, but some areas feel too empty. Strangely enough, Snake Eater manages to make its environments feel more alive than The Phantom Pain does in certain open areas. In this case, a smaller world that feels alive leaves a stronger impression than a huge world you mostly just pass through.

The impression that remains is that Kojima was limited in this area and could not fully develop his vision for an open world and a more alive, refined, and interactive Mother Base.

Even so, MGSV is still an extraordinary experience. The tactical freedom is outstanding, the stealth is sharp, the number of possible approaches is huge, and very few games manage to combine gameplay, management, and narrative in such a unique way.

Every Metal Gear fan should play it. After going for 100% and unlocking every achievement, it becomes even clearer how much the game rewards dedication and attention to detail.

MGSV leaves you feeling that it could have been even greater, but what is here is already more than enough to be unforgettable.


PT-BR resumo:

MGSV é uma obra-prima de gameplay, ambição narrativa e liberdade tática. Mesmo com sinais de uma produção conturbada e elementos comerciais como as MB Coins, o núcleo da experiência permanece forte. A Mother Base e o mundo aberto poderiam ser mais vivos, mas o que existe aqui já é inesquecível. O jogo possui legendas em PT-BR, mas não conta com dublagem em português. Todo fã de Metal Gear deveria jogar. Depois de buscar o 100% e liberar todas as conquistas, fica ainda mais claro o quanto o jogo recompensa dedicação e atenção aos detalhes.

An amazing sendoff to the series that defined stealth-action gameplay. Gives you the freedom of open-world sandbox creativity with the tools of a linear game, and lets you interpret it as you wish. Also redefines and perfects the Mother Base system born in Peace Walker by adding a manual platform progression system, and separating the Analyzer scanning speed from it's development grade.

The FOB 1v1-style multiplayer is still yet something I've been able to tackle, however it's not very active anymore apart from people who've been playing years past on the same data. Metal Gear Online 3 is also dead, but could still be a real fun time with a gaggle of folks. Runs buttery smooth on anything past 2019, and can look stunning when used with Reshade (after some messing around, of course.)

All in all definitely worth picking up as a new player or longtime fan.
one of the best tactical games i have ever played...the game is so immersive and fun

Playing MGSV was a fantastic experience. The controls are incredibly fluid which is a great benefit to the player. Completely disregard the diehard MGS fans that spout all the negativity for this game, because they're overlooking some key things for other players. Play it because it's good and fun. My entire platinum journey was a fantastically phenomenal experience.

An incredibly fun stealth game. The gameplay is the best in the series, and the genre.

I'd recommend playing MGS3: Snake Eater first, since it's the 2nd best in the series, and serves as the backstory to this game. If you really want to skip straight to this game, just make sure to listen to all the audio-logs as they unlock, and they'll catch you up to speed with the story well enough.

Make sure to buy the Definitive Edition of MGSV, since that includes MGSV: Ground Zeroes, which is a short intro level meant to be played before MGSV: The Phantom Pain.

Honestly I was expecting a lot more from a game that was so highly praised by people.

I think this was pretty terrible from start to finish.

Why is this game constantly making me upgrade things that I can't use? Every time you try and experiment with anything the game happily wags its finger in your face saying 'NO NO NO, DO IT PROPERLY, DO IT OPTIMALLY OR NOT AT ALL', it doesn't want you to have fun, it just wants to you to do it in the safest most boring way possible or else you get sent back 40 mins to the last checkpoint with a message asking if you'd like the chicken hat. 

If I am trying to set up something fun and I am dying, why would I ever want to suddenly change all the behaviours of every enemy in the game?

Isn't it basic game design that there should be as little time between the player and getting back into the game as possible after a death? Like that's game design 101, so why am I clicking this pop-up again and again and again and again? I'm trying to have fun, I don't want to just use the sniper rifle and nothing else for the whole game.

Who even are these characters? Why can't they stop being so horrendously cringe for 5 seconds? Will there ever be a point in the hours of cutscenes that I actually care about them, at all? Nope. They are completely insufferable and you won't miss them when they're gone.

This game is Mercenaries: playground of destruction if it discouraged you from purchasing new items.
This game is Hitman if it didn't have any manual saving system so you can't try anything.

This game has lots of interesting parts, but they aren't put together in a cohesive, fun or well thought out way.

-Graphics: It's 2026... I know it, BUT GRAPHICS are still a thing here, I love it, some stuff are like 480p like some posters or stuff on the floor... but a game from 2015?
4k with my GTX 1070

-Art Direction: Quiet, The Skulls, Skull Face, Kazuhira Miller, the child soldiers, Ocelot, soldiers from old Mother base from peace walker (This was pure for fans), Mini Psyco mantis, Mini Liquid, Man of fire, Sahelanthropus, Huey, Boss, Big boss, Code Talker,
The buildings, the environment, the animals, omfg

- Audio: I love the music and the tension audio of this game, the sound makes me walk in lot of missions to feel the moment 

- Performance: I play with a GTX 1070 and a r7 5700g 36gb ram, I play this game 4k full, I love the performance

- Content: I got 330 Hours, What can I say? I'm bored? No no, I want to finish the achievements and walk around the maps, capturing every single soldier, Listening the talk between soldiers, asking Quiet to shoot a grenade...  

Konami, give to Kojima his Baby, we need to end this history

Why are we still here? Just to suffer? Every night, I can feel my leg... And my arm... even my fingers... The body I've lost... the comrades I've lost... won't stop hurting... It's like they're all still there.
This game made my snake very solid, extremely replayable.
